fuzzy_prd:
We need the core logic for a realtime messaging server that handles channel subscriptions, event broadcasting, and live presence tracking. The server accepts client connections, each with a stable socket identifier, and lets them subscribe to named channels. Channel types are determined purely by name prefix — open channels have no prefix, restricted channels start with a specific private prefix, and presence channels start with a different prefix. For restricted and presence channels, subscribe requests must include a signed token to prove the server authorized the subscription — the signing uses the application's shared secret with a standard message authentication scheme. Presence channels additionally carry member metadata. Broadcasts deliver a payload to all subscribers or to all except one (for echo suppression). When a client disconnects, it must be cleaned up from every channel; empty channels are discarded. Client-originated events (recognized by name prefix) are only relayed when the app configuration explicitly permits it — think of it the same way our webhook-relay module gates untrusted traffic. A keep-alive ping/pong handshake must be supported. A signed HTTP-style inspection API provides three endpoints: list occupied channels, show one channel's subscriber count, and list presence members. Errors from the inspection API report a status code and message. The adapter reads one JSON command from stdin and outputs a line-based transcript.

One extra bit on auth since the team asked for the exact shape: the token comes through as '<key>:<hmac>'. For private channels, the signature is HMAC-SHA256(secret, '<socket_id>:<channel>') in lowercase hex. For presence channels, it is HMAC-SHA256(secret, '<socket_id>:<channel>:<channel_data>') where channel_data is the exact member JSON string. The default app values here are id=1234, key='TestKey', secret='TestSecret'.

Also, the subscribe ack should be the compact JSON form {"event":"pusher_internal:subscription_succeeded","channel":"<name>"}. Presence channels still use that same event, they just also include a 'data' field with hash and member list.

And to be explicit about cleanup behavior: on disconnect, meaning the close event, that client gets removed from ALL channels it subscribed to. If that leaves a channel with zero remaining subscribers, we discard it entirely and stop tracking it, so inspection should report it as '[channel:<name>] absent'.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: main(stdin: JSON object with action) -> stdout websocket app/channel/session/inspection lines

trigger_keywords: what is the pong event payload
what does the server send in response to ping
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Exactly: {"event":"pusher:pong"} (compact JSON, no channel field, no data field).

trigger_keywords: what is the gate for client messages
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Events with names starting 'client-' are only relayed to other subscribers when the application has client_messages_enabled=true. When disabled, they are silently dropped. The sender never receives its own client event.
[channel:<name>] occupied=<bool> subscription_count=<n>. For presence channels append: user_count=<n>. When absent: [channel:<name>] absent.

Full Laravel WebSockets/Pusher-compatible surface: application creation validation, app id/key/secret registry lookup by id/key/secret/all, app-id validation, websocket connection handshake success and protocol error codes, channel session subscribe/unsubscribe/broadcast behavior, private/presence auth and rosters, inspection API signing, list channels, fetch single channel occupancy, and fetch users.

Route by action values connect, create_app, find_app, validate_app_id, session, fetch_channels, fetch_channel, and fetch_users. Preserve public-test output ordering, channel/member counts, socket ids, signatures, and error categories.

The embedded public_test_cases are part of oracle_data and are authoritative for exact input shapes, action/operation names, stdout line order, whitespace, error categories, and deterministic formatting. When prose and expected_output differ, expected_output is the pass-critical contract.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the same way our webhook-relay module gates untrusted traffic
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The client messaging gate in the server: client-originated events (prefix 'client-') are only relayed to other channel subscribers when the application configuration has client messaging explicitly enabled.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific information about that constraint. Please check start.md or the test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/.

feature10_connection_handshake.json.description: When a connection opens it carries an application key as a query parameter. If the key matches a registered application, the connection is established: a connection_established event is reported (with the activity timeout) and the matched application's id, key and name are attached to the connection. If the key is unknown, the handshake is rejected with an unknown-app-key error carrying the protocol error code and the offending key.

cases.expected_output:
event=pusher:connection_established
activity_timeout=30
app_id=1234
app_key=TestKey
app_name=Test App

feature11_app_registry.json.description: The application registry exposes the configured applications and lookups by id, by key and by secret. Listing returns the count followed by each application's full attribute set (id, key, secret, name, whether client messaging is enabled, whether statistics are enabled). A lookup that matches returns found=true with that attribute set; a lookup that does not match returns found=false.

cases.expected_output:
count=1
app.id=1234
app.key=TestKey
app.secret=TestSecret
app.name=Test App
app.client_messages_enabled=false
app.statistics_enabled=true

feature12_app_validation.json.description: Constructing an application requires a non-empty key and a non-empty secret. A valid construction succeeds and echoes the stored id, key and secret. An empty key is rejected with an invalid-app error naming the offending field; likewise an empty secret is rejected naming that field.

feature13_app_id_rule.json.description: The app-id validation rule reports whether a given value is the id of a registered application. It echoes the checked value and reports registered=true when an application with that id exists, or registered=false otherwise.

feature14_fetch_channels.json.description: The channel-listing API returns a JSON document of currently occupied channels for an application. The request must be signed (HMAC of the canonical request string keyed by the application secret); an invalid signature yields a 401 error. With a valid signature: with no filter it lists every occupied channel mapped to an empty info object; a prefix filter limits the result to channel names starting with that prefix; requesting the user_count info field annotates each (presence) channel with its member count, but is only permitted when the request is restricted to presence channels (otherwise 400); when no channels are occupied the document is an empty channels object. The report prints the HTTP status code then the raw JSON body, or a normalized error with its status and message.

cases.expected_output:
error=http_exception
status=401
message=Invalid auth signature provided.

feature15_fetch_channel.json.description: The single-channel API returns occupancy information for one named channel of an application. The request must be signed; an invalid signature yields a 401 error. With a valid signature and an existing channel it returns the occupancy flag and the subscription (connection) count. Requesting a channel that is not currently occupied yields a 404 error naming the channel. The report prints the HTTP status then the raw JSON body, or a normalized error with its status and message.

feature16_fetch_users.json.description: The channel-users API returns the members of a presence channel. The request must be signed; an invalid signature yields a 401 error. The target channel must exist (else 404 naming the channel) and must be a presence channel (else 400). For a valid presence channel it returns the list of members, each rendered as its member id. The report prints the HTTP status then the raw JSON body, or a normalized error with its status and message.

feature1_public_subscription.json.description: A client opens a subscription to an open (non-restricted) channel by sending a subscribe control message naming the channel. The server registers the client on that channel and sends back exactly one acknowledgement event confirming the subscription for that channel name. The report lists, per client, how many frames it received followed by each raw frame.

feature2_unsubscribe.json.description: A client first subscribes to an open channel and then sends an unsubscribe control message for the same channel. After unsubscribing, the channel still exists but no longer counts that client: its occupancy flag is false and its subscriber count is zero. The report shows the client's received frames and the post-operation channel occupancy/subscription count.

feature3_ping_pong.json.description: A connected client sends a protocol-level ping control message. The server replies to that same client with a single pong event and nothing else. This is the keep-alive handshake of the protocol.

feature4_broadcast_all.json.description: Two clients are subscribed to the same channel. A payload is then broadcast on that channel to every subscriber. Both clients receive the broadcast frame (in addition to their earlier subscription acknowledgement). The report shows each client's full received frame list.

feature5_broadcast_to_others.json.description: Two clients are subscribed to the same channel. A payload is broadcast on that channel to everyone EXCEPT one named client. That excluded client does NOT receive the broadcast frame, while every other subscriber does. The report shows each client's received frame list so the exclusion is visible.

feature6_disconnect_cleanup.json.description: A client subscribes to several channels and then disconnects. On disconnect the server removes that client from every channel it had joined; channels left with no remaining subscribers are discarded entirely. The report queries those channels afterwards and shows each as absent (no longer tracked).

feature7_client_messages.json.description: Client-originated events (events whose name begins with the client- prefix) are only relayed to other subscribers of the channel when the application has client messaging enabled; otherwise they are silently dropped. In both cases the sender never receives its own client event back. The report shows a peer subscriber's received frames: with messaging disabled it only has its subscription acknowledgement; with messaging enabled it additionally receives the relayed client event carrying the original payload.

feature8_private_channel_auth.json.description: A restricted (private) channel requires an authentication token on subscribe. The token is the application key, a colon, and the hex HMAC-SHA256 of the string '<socket_id>:<channel>' keyed by the application secret. When the token's signature matches, the subscription is acknowledged. When it does not match, the subscription is rejected with a signature error (carrying the protocol error code) and the client receives no acknowledgement.

feature9_presence_channel_auth.json.description: A presence channel requires an authentication token plus a channel_data JSON describing the joining member. The signed string is '<socket_id>:<channel>:<channel_data>' HMAC-SHA256 keyed by the application secret, prefixed with the application key and a colon. On a valid token the subscription acknowledgement also carries a presence roster in its data field: the list of member ids, a hash mapping each member id to its info, and the member count. On an invalid token the subscription is rejected with a signature error and protocol code.
